food_subgroup: Braised pork dishes; stuffed bread / flatbread sandwiches

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for braised pork sandwich or roujiamo. FOODON_03302050 (pork-containing food product) is used as the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Pig meat products) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for braised pork sandwich or roujiamo in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for braised pork sandwich or roujiamo. SNOMED CT does not have a specific food concept for this dish.

main_ingredients: spices (star anise, cinnamon, bay leaf, Sichuan pepper)

serving.natural_language_portion: One Classic Braised Pork Sandwich typically weighs approximately 200 g, comprising a filled flatbread bun with a generous portion of braised pork belly and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, belly (side), raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168375); Bread, white, commercially prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 325871); composite estimate for roujiamo-style braised pork sandwich
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Classic Braised Pork Sandwich or roujiamo exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing: (1) USDA SR Legacy pork belly (braised, FDC ID 168375 adjusted for cooking loss), (2) USDA SR Legacy white wheat flatbread/bun, and (3) soy sauce and spice contributions. Approximate composition by weight: ~55% braised pork belly, ~40% wheat flatbread, ~5% sauce/condiments. Values are per 100 g of the assembled sandwich. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white wheat bread and pork-containing mixed meals — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for braised pork sandwich
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Classic Braised Pork Sandwich or roujiamo. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate component being a white wheat flatbread bun (GI ~70 per University of Sydney GI Database for white bread) moderated by the substantial protein and fat content of braised pork belly (~55% of sandwich weight), which is well-established to lower the glycemic response of mixed meals. The composite GI of 55 is consistent with published mixed-meal GI attenuation data. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 41.0) / 100 = 22.6 (serving = 200 g; carbohydrate per serving = 20.5 × 2 = 41.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~960 mg per sandwich serving) from soy sauce braising liquid and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this sandwich due to its high sodium load (~960 mg per serving, approximately 42% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced-soy-sauce preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) but moderate glycemic load per serving (~22.6) due to refined wheat flatbread, partially offset by high protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this sandwich in moderation; while the GI is low, the refined wheat bun contributes to a moderate-to-high glycemic load of ~22.6 per sandwich, so portion control and pairing with non-starchy vegetables is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~5.3 g per 100 g; ~10.6 g per serving) and high sodium from pork belly and soy sauce braising
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its high saturated fat content (~10.6 g per serving, exceeding 50% of the recommended daily limit) and high sodium, both of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Classic Braised Pork Sandwich (卤肉夹馍 / 滷肉夾饃, Lǔ Ròu Jiā Mó) is a Chinese street-food sandwich originating from Shaanxi Province, China, and widely adapted across Chinese communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish consists of slow-braised pork belly — cooked for several hours in a spiced soy-sauce broth containing star anise, cinnamon, bay leaf, Sichuan pepper, garlic, ginger, rice wine, and sugar — stuffed generously into a baked wheat flatbread bun (mo or jia mo). The braising process renders the pork belly tender and richly flavoured, with a deep umami-sweet-savoury profile and a melt-in-the-mouth texture. The flatbread is soft inside with a lightly crisp exterior. Optional additions include green chilli, coriander, or pickled vegetables. Nutritionally, the sandwich is energy-dense (~268 kcal per 100 g; ~536 kcal per sandwich), with significant protein (~14 g/100 g) and fat (~15 g/100 g, of which ~5 g is saturated), and moderate carbohydrate (~20 g/100 g) from the wheat bun. Sodium content is high (~480 mg/100 g) due to soy sauce. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low), moderated by the protein and fat content of the pork. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat and sodium), and Type 2 Diabetes (moderate glycemic load). It is not halal due to pork content. In Singapore, it is served as a casual meal or snack and is associated with Chinese-style comfort food.
common_user_queries: What is a braised pork sandwich?
How many calories in a braised pork sandwich?
Can diabetics eat braised pork sandwich?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Classic Braised Pork Sandwich or roujiamo was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy data for braised pork belly and white wheat bread, weighted by approximate ingredient proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for white bread moderated by mixed-meal protein and fat effects. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for braised pork belly (FDC ID 168375) and white wheat bread/bun (FDC ID 325871), weighted at approximately 55% pork belly and 40% flatbread by cooked weight, with 5% sauce contribution. No direct database entry for roujiamo or Classic Braised Pork Sandwich was identified in any approved primary source. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. 4. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported with sufficient confidence in the composite estimate. 5. GI is estimated at 55 (low) using an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for white bread (GI ~70), attenuated by the high protein and fat content of braised pork belly in a mixed-meal context; this is a defensible analogous estimate, not a direct measurement. 6. The food is not halal due to pork content. 7. The field dataed in the source data (Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles 兰亭序) primarily serves Lanzhou-style noodles; the braised pork sandwich is a secondary menu item consistent with northern Chinese cuisine. 8. Cuisine tags in the source data include 'Drinks & Beverages' and 'Noodles', which are not applicable to this food item and have been disregarded. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and carry medium confidence.
